title: Class 8: Unemployment, Recessions, the Keynesian Model, and Fiscal Policy
coverageSummary: Unemployment measurement (labor force, unemployment rate, participation rate) with real BLS employment-status data tables; costs and duration of unemployment; frictional, cyclical, and structural unemployment, including a minimum-wage labor-market diagram; business cycles and recession/expansion/depression definitions; potential vs. actual output and the output gap formula; the natural rate of unemployment, cyclical unemployment, and Okun's Law; sticky prices as the short-run rationale for persistent output gaps; the Keynesian model (planned aggregate expenditure, the consumption function, a worked short-run-equilibrium example, the Keynesian cross, and the income-expenditure multiplier); and fiscal policy (stabilization policy, taxes/transfers, supply-side effects, deficit spending, flexibility limits, automatic stabilizers, and the 2008-09 stimulus acts).
reliabilityNotes: All 56 slides' bullet text and speaker notes extracted cleanly via python-pptx. A handful of slides (title/photo cards, a generic business-cycle wave graphic, a FRED output-gap/NROU chart, the Keynesian-cross plot itself, an appendix multiplier slide, and a bare 'Monetary Policy (Next class)' divider) are diagram/photo-only and were not used as a standalone source of new content; where a diagram's underlying concept (e.g., the minimum-wage labor-market floor, or the Keynesian-cross 45-degree-line logic) is also explained in adjacent bullet text, that bullet text is the actual grounding for the corresponding question, not the image itself. Slides 6-8's real BLS employment data (June 2026/2020/2019) extracted as clean text and confirm the unemployment-rate/participation-rate formulas, but per the no-verbatim/no-mad-libs rule, this bank's formula questions use freshly invented figures rather than these exact real-world numbers. No question in this source's set required a needsReview flag.

title: Class 9: Central Banks, the Federal Reserve, and Monetary Policy
coverageSummary: The Federal Reserve's structure (12 districts, Board of Governors, FOMC) and founding objective; the Fed's tools for controlling the money supply (open-market operations, discount window lending, the reserve requirement, interest on reserves, quantitative easing); money demand (opportunity cost, determinants, the downward-sloping money demand curve and its shifters); equilibrium in the money market and how the Fed shifts the vertical money-supply curve to hit a target nominal interest rate; the federal funds rate; the Fed's control over the real interest rate (r = i - pi) and monetary policy's transmission to consumption and investment, including a worked Keynesian numeric example with r in the spending equations; three additional transmission channels (bank lending, asset price, exchange rate); inflation's effect on the stock market; policy reaction functions and the Taylor rule; and the 2008-09 financial crisis's monetary policy response (near-zero fed funds rate, unconventional monetary policy/quantitative easing, and the zero lower bound).
reliabilityNotes: This is a 46-page PDF export (filename marked '_Preliminary', i.e. a work-in-progress deck) extracted with pdftotext -layout. Roughly a third of the pages are diagram/photo-only with 0-2 lines of extractable text (the MS/MD shift diagrams beyond their bare axis labels, a Fed-leaders composite photo, an FOMC-minutes screenshot, an 'Expanding the Fed's Balance Sheet' chart, and a Covid-19 policy-response chart); none of these was used as the source of any factual claim -- every graph/diagram question is grounded in the deck's own surrounding bullet text (e.g., 'Supply of money is vertical,' 'Fed increases the money supply to MS'... interest rates decrease to i''), not in reading unreadable chart pixels. The deck's agenda lists 'Fiscal policy' as its first topic, but no slide between the title/agenda pages and the 'Federal Reserve' section has any extractable fiscal-policy text (confirmed via a per-page text-line-count check) -- this is consistent with the file's preliminary status, since class8 already teaches fiscal policy in full. No fiscal-policy question in this bank cites class9; all cite class8, which does carry that content. No question in this source's set required a needsReview flag.
